questions.question: Explain the limitations of physical clocks in distributed systems. Why do we need logical clocks?
keyPoints: Physical clocks drift due to oscillator variations
Network delays prevent perfect synchronization even with NTP
Logical clocks provide ordering based on causality rather than real time
Physical time cannot guarantee precise ordering of events

questions.question: Define the happened-before relation (→) in distributed systems and state its properties.
keyPoints: Same process: if a and b in same process and a before b, then a→b
Message send-receive: if a is send and b is receive, then a→b
Transitive: if a→b and b→c, then a→c
Properties: reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive (partial order)

questions.question: How does Lamport's logical clock work when a process receives a message?
keyPoints: Receiver obtains timestamp T from incoming message
Updates local counter to max(local_counter, T)
Increments the counter by 1 for the receive event
Ensures causality is preserved across processes

questions.question: Explain the difference between causal ordering and total ordering of messages.
keyPoints: Causal order: if send(M1)→send(M2), then delivery respects this
Total order: all receivers deliver messages in exactly the same sequence
Total order implies causal order but not vice versa
Total order is stronger and more expensive to implement

questions.question: How are vector clocks used to detect concurrent events?
keyPoints: Two events are concurrent if neither VC1 ≤ VC2 nor VC2 ≤ VC1
For VC comparison: V1 ≤ V2 iff V1[i] ≤ V2[i] for all i
Concurrent events have incomparable vector clocks
This allows systems to detect conflicts in distributed data stores

questions.question: What are the advantages of vector clocks over Lamport timestamps?
keyPoints: Vector clocks can distinguish concurrent from causally related events
Lamport timestamps only provide total ordering
Vector clocks capture full causal dependency information
Vector clocks enable conflict detection in eventually consistent systems

questions.question: Explain how logical time is used in distributed debugging with an example.
keyPoints: Developers can trace potential cause-effect relationships
Events can be ordered to identify which could have influenced others
Lamport timestamps provide total order for log analysis
Vector clocks identify which events could NOT have caused others (concurrent)

questions.question: Explain Lamport's logical clocks in detail. Describe the algorithm for timestamp assignment and discuss its limitations.
keyPoints: Each process maintains counter initialized to 0
For every event at process, increment counter
When sending message, include timestamp
When receiving message, set counter to max(local, received) + 1
This provides total ordering of all events
Limitation: cannot capture causality - cannot distinguish concurrent events
Limitation: requires additional messages overhead
All processes can agree on total order but lose causal information

questions.question: Explain vector clocks in detail. How do they overcome the limitations of Lamport timestamps? Explain with an example showing update rules and comparison.
keyPoints: Each process maintains vector of n counters (n = number of processes)
On local event at Pi: increment V[i]
On sending: include entire vector
On receiving from Pj: V[k] = max(V[k], received[j]) for all k, then increment V[i]
Comparison: V1 < V2 if V1[i] ≤ V2[i] for all i and at least one strict
Concurrent if neither ≤ the other
Overcomes Lamport: can detect causality vs concurrency
Example with 2 processes and message passing showing vector evolution

questions.question: Design a distributed collaborative editing system using logical clocks. Explain how you would use either Lamport timestamps or vector clocks to handle concurrent edits and maintain consistency.
keyPoints: Use vector clocks to detect concurrent edits
Each user's document has version vector
When receiving edit, compare version vectors
If concurrent: flag for conflict resolution or use last-writer-wins
If causally related: apply in order
Lamport would give total order but lose conflict info
Vector clocks allow detecting conflicts before merging
Can implement operational transformation or CRDT approaches
Trade-offs: memory overhead vs causality detection

questions.question: Compare causal broadcast and atomic (total order) broadcast. Explain when each would be used and how logical clocks enable their implementation.
keyPoints: Causal broadcast: delivery respects happens-before
Atomic broadcast: all receivers deliver in same order
Implementation using Lamport: assign timestamps, deliver in timestamp order for atomic
For causal: use vector clocks, deliver only when prerequisites satisfied
Causal is weaker: less overhead, sufficient for many applications
Atomic required for distributed databases, state machine replication
Examples: causal for chat messages, atomic for database commits
